title: Expected Value Thinking
subtopics: When EV fails: fat tails and ruin

slides.text:
Expected value = sum of each outcome's value times its probability.
It's the long-run average payoff if you repeat the choice many times.

Why it works
- EV collapses uncertainty to one metric for comparing options
- Linearity means small-probability large payoffs can outweigh many modest outcomes

Formula
EV = Σ (p_i x v_i)
Example: 1% chance of $10,000, 99% $0 -> EV = 0.01x10,000 = $100

How to apply
1) List relevant outcomes
2) Estimate probabilities from data or ranges
3) Assign values (money or utility)
4) Compute EV and compare, noting variance and constraints

Startup investing
Invest $10k for 1% of a firm. If exit = $100M with 1% chance, your share = $1M.
EV ≈ 0.01x$1,000,000 - $10,000 = $0; wins across many bets drive returns.

Value of information (medical test)
Compute EV with and without the test: if avoided harm's EV > test cost, testing is worth it.
Compare decision EVs, not just accuracy.

EV vs expected utility
- EV uses raw payoffs
- Expected utility converts payoffs to personal utility for risk aversion
Takeaway: positive EV can be rejected if utility loss from downside dominates

Definition

Opportunity cost = value of the best alternative you give up when choosing. Identify the realistic next-best option before deciding.

Mechanism

- Resources (time, money, attention) are scarce.
- Choosing A prevents B.
- Opportunity cost = benefit(B) minus benefit(A) or simply benefit(B) vs baseline.

Shadow pricing your time

- Put a dollar value on a blocked resource.
- Example: billable rate = $150/hr.
- If you skip billable work, cost = $150 for each hour spent elsewhere.

Hiring vs DIY

- Engineer costs $60/hr or you spend 5 hrs at $200/hr value.
- Opportunity cost of doing it yourself = 5 x $200 = $1,000.
- If hiring saves under $1,000 in value, hire.

Cost of delay

- Early launch = $10k/week expected profit.
- A 2-week delay costs ~ $20k in lost opportunity.
- Add this to development trade-offs when prioritizing backlog.

How to use it

1) List realistic alternatives.
2) Estimate marginal benefits in a common unit (money, hours, EV).
3) Compare chosen option to best alternative using ranges.

Common misuse: Sunk-cost trap

- Sunk cost = past money already lost; ignore it for current choices.
- Don't keep failing projects just because you spent $1M already.

Definition

- First-order: the immediate intended result.
- Second-order: indirect consequences from that result.
- Third-order: consequences of the second-order. Map at least one level deeper.

Mechanism

- Action changes behavior or conditions (first order).
- That change alters incentives, constraints, or info (second order).
- Those shifts trigger adaptive responses (third order).

How to think in second orders

1) State the intended effect.
2) Ask who gains/loses and which incentives shift.
3) Predict stakeholder responses and feedback loops.

Example: rent caps

- First: tenants pay lower rent.
- Second: landlords cut maintenance or exit rentals.
- Third: housing quality and supply fall, worsening affordability.

Example: email reminders

- First: compliance rises.
- Second: people batch or delay work to avoid reminders.
- Third: throughput drops and behavior becomes gameable.

Quick checklist

- What incentives change?
- Who can act to capture them?
- Any capacity limits or bottlenecks?
- Will effects scale or be delayed?

Common misuse

- Don't invent elaborate chains.
- Check whether actors are able and motivated to respond.
- If not, second-order risk is low.

title: Premortem Planning: Find Failures Before You Commit

slides.text:
Premortem planning
Imagine a future failure now so you can prevent it before you commit.

Definition
Imagine a vivid failure, then work backward to map causes, assumptions, and measurable stop-losses you need to test.

Why it works
- Forces concrete failure stories from vague worries
- Turns assumptions into tests, mitigations, and binary kill criteria

Quick setup (20-45m)
1) State the commitment
2) Assume failure six months later and write 3-5 specific failures
3) Cluster, prioritize, define tests and kill criteria

Example failures (B2B analytics beta)
- Onboarding too slow -> churn
- APIs miss key fields -> product unusable
- Sales cycle is 9 months, not 3 -> burn through cash

From failure to action
- Onboarding: measure TTFU; pause if <40% reach it in 2 weeks
- APIs: 3 pilot integrations; stop if >2 custom patches each
- Sales: require 2 signed LOIs before rollout

Red-team twist
- Assign one person to argue "this will still fail"
- Their job: force hard conditions (metrics, verified integrations, contracts) before you proceed

Decide which choices you can undo and which lock you in.
Spend effort where reversibility is low.

Reversible: undo with low cost or delay (two-way door).
Irreversible: undo is costly, slow, or impossible (one-way door).

- Why it matters: reversible choices let you experiment and learn cheaply.
- Irreversible choices lock in outcomes and risk - need better info and alignment.

- What does 'undo' require: time, money, reputation?
- Can we run a small experiment first?
- Who must consent before we escalate?

- Reversibility budget: limit your scarce irreversible moves.
- Use experiments and safe defaults for most decisions; reserve irreversible moves for long-term leverage.

- Feature flag rollout = two-way door: instant rollback, quick test.
- Replacing core DB = one-way door: migration risk, long downtime, heavier review.

- Hiring one person is mostly reversible: reassign or exit at modest cost.
- Major org reorg is often irreversible: changes reporting, pay, and customer interfaces - needs wide alignment.

title: Decision hygiene - separate judgment from execution

slides.text:
Decision hygiene - make choices cleaner, repeatable, and auditable.
Treat decisions as processes you can inspect and improve.

Definition

Decision hygiene = deliberate practices that separate judgment from execution.
Make assumptions explicit and structure info so signal outweighs noise.

Why it works

Biases and pressure skew judgment. Explicit steps (record options, score criteria, independent forecasts) reduce anchoring, hindsight bias, and groupthink.

Core checklist (before major choices)

- State decision and deadline
- List alternatives and key assumptions
- Pick objective criteria, record confidence, assign decision vs. execution roles

Decision memo template

- Question + 3 options max
- Key assumptions (with probabilities)
- Evaluation matrix, chosen option, rationale, owner and timeline

Product launch example

- PM writes the memo
- Eng scores feasibility; Sales scores demand; Finance scores margin
- Track adoption vs. assumptions and revisit if adoption <5% in 60 days

Hiring example

- Panel submits separate written assessments before discussion
- Hiring manager weights skill, culture, ramp time and decides from the documented trade-offs

Definition
- Forecast by finding similar past cases (a reference class).
- Use the empirical outcome distribution (outside view), not your unique story.

Mechanism
1) Pick a measurable outcome (cost, time).
2) Assemble a relevant reference class.
3) Build the outcome distribution.
4) Place your case by percentile and report risk bands.

Situation
- City team estimates 3 years and $400M for a new light-rail (inside view).
- Apply reference class forecasting to ground that estimate in real outcomes.

Pick the reference class
- Find ~30 comparable urban rail projects in similar economies.
- Filter for new light-rail only; balance closeness with having enough data.

Use the distribution
- Historical overruns: median +35%, 75th +60%, 90th +110%.
- Choose a cautious percentile (e.g., 75th) => $400M x 1.6 = $640M and report risk band to 90th.

Why it works
- The outside view captures common surprises and systematic bias.
- It replaces optimistic stories with empirical calibration from many analogs.

Common failures
- Wrong or mixed reference class (e.g., include subways).
- Cherry-picking favorable examples.
- Over-adjusting with inside-view optimism instead of using the empirical distribution.

Definition

- Rule for revising a prior probability into a posterior when you see new evidence.
- Combine prior belief with the evidence' likelihood to get the posterior.

Mechanism

- Start with a prior (base rate + uncertainty).
- For each hypothesis, compute P(evidence | hypothesis).
- Multiply prior x likelihood and normalize to sum to 1.

Compact formula

- Posterior ∝ Prior x Likelihood.
- Two-hypothesis form: P(H|E)=P(E|H)P(H)/[P(E|H)P(H)+P(E|¬H)P(¬H)].
- Or use odds: posterior odds = prior odds x likelihood ratio.

Medical test example

- Prior (prevalence) = 1%.
- Sensitivity 95%, false positive 5%.
- Posterior after a positive = 0.95x0.01 / [0.95x0.01+0.05x0.99] ≈ 16%.

Decision implication

- A 16% posterior often doesn't justify invasive, costly action.
- Prefer cheaper confirmatory tests or a threshold-based rule tied to costs and benefits.

Prior sensitivity

- Vary plausible priors and see how posteriors change.
- If results swing widely, gather stronger evidence or postpone irreversible choices.

Weak evidence

- Small likelihood ratios (≈1) move posteriors little, even from extreme priors.
- Stack independent weak signals before making big bets.

Definition
- Confidence = your stated probability for an outcome.
- Calibration = how often those probabilities actually occur (e.g., 90% statements happen ~90% of the time).

Why it matters
- Well-calibrated probs let teams combine judgments and manage risk.
- Overconfidence drives surprise, missed contingencies, and poor aggregation.

Key pieces
- Point probabilities (e.g., 70%) and interval statements (e.g., 90% interval).
- Outcomes to compare and a scoring method (calibration curve or Brier score).

How it works
- Bin predictions by stated probability, measure actual hit rate per bin.
- Plot calibration curve: on 45° = calibrated; below = overconfident; above = underconfident.

Example
- Rep gives 100 deals labeled 70% likely.
- If ~70 close -> calibrated; 90 -> underconfident; 50 -> overconfident.

90% intervals
- A correct 90% interval contains the true value about 90% of the time.
- Use for continuous quantities and check coverage frequency.

Overconfidence audit
1) Log predictions and confidences.
2) Compute hit rates by bin.
3) Adjust probability mapping or widen intervals to improve calibration.

Make messy choices predictable.
Use weights to compare, vetoes for must-haves, and Pareto thinking to show real tradeoffs.

The challenge
- Decisions depend on multiple competing criteria (cost, speed, quality, risk).
- Multi-criteria methods measure, combine, and reveal which options are truly better.

Core mechanisms
- Weighted scoring: normalize scores, apply importance weights, sum to rank.
- Veto: eliminate options that fail must-have thresholds.
- Pareto: keep non-dominated options only.

Hiring example
- Rate candidates on impact, ramp, salary.
- Weights favor ramp -> B wins.
- A veto (salary cap) removes C.
- Pareto: A and B are non-dominated, so pick which tradeoff to accept.

Roadmap example
- Score features by value, cost, and risk.
- Weights rank features.
- A risk veto blocks risky ones.
- Pareto shows features that are strictly better value-for-cost.

How to apply
1) Pick 3-5 decision-aligned criteria.
2) Normalize scores (e.g., 0-10).
3) Set veto thresholds.
4) Weight and sum.
5) Check Pareto front before reweighting.

Tradeoff triangle (quick view)
- Choose three axes (speed / cost / scope).
- Plot options: edges = explicit tradeoffs, interior = balanced.
- Use for fast visual sense of trade choices.

title: Narrative fallacy - how stories mislead judgment

slides.text:
Definition

We make neat stories after events to explain them. Plausible narratives replace messy facts, trimming randomness and ignoring alternative causes.

Mechanism

After an outcome we seek a causal chain. We fill gaps with plausible links, discard noise, and become overconfident in the story.

Signal vs noise

Stories hide variability and uncertainty.
They turn rare or random events into "inevitable" outcomes and promote one dominant explanation.

Example - business pivot

A viral post prompts leaders to claim market validation.
They ignore selection, other failures, and treat a fluke as a reliable signal.

Example - financial crashes

Commentators pick a simple villain ("greed" or "regulation").
That sidesteps the messy mix of leverage, liquidity, psychology, and chance.

Misuse - post-hoc rationalization

Treating correlation as a recipe: crediting a rebrand for growth while ignoring macro trends and product changes.

How to guard against it

1) Start data-first: ask what evidence the story predicts.
2) Test alternatives and seek disconfirming data.
3) Quantify uncertainty and check out-of-sample results.

**Fat tails = big surprises**

- Extremes far more likely than Gaussian
- Power law: P(X>x) ∝ x^-α
- Small change in α alters which moments (mean, var) exist

**Why it breaks intuition**

- Tail events can dominate sums and averages
- If α ≤ 2 variance is infinite; if α ≤ 1 mean may be undefined
- Law of large numbers fails to help

**Scaling intuition**

- P(X>x) ~ x^-α so doubling x multiplies tail prob by 2^-α
- For α≈1 the tail falls very slowly and extremes shape totals

**Wealth example**

- A few billionaires shift the mean wealth
- Mean misleads; median shows typical experience
- Policy based on means misses most people

**Venture returns**

- A tiny fraction of startups deliver most returns
- Portfolios are tail-driven: one home run can dominate results
- Simple diversification by count may not help

**Natural hazards & insurance**

- Earthquake and hurricane losses have fat tails
- Gaussian pricing underestimates reserves and overstates certainty
- Prepare for rare, massive losses

**Detecting fat tails**

- CCDF on log-log looks linear for power laws
- Hill estimator estimates α
- Practical sign: extremes repeatedly dominate totals and means jump with new data

What it is
- An explicit buffer between capacity and expected load.
- Covers estimation error, rare events, and unknown unknowns.

How it works
- Estimate required capacity.
- Identify uncertainties (model, measurement, tails).
- Add a buffer sized to cut failure probability to an acceptable level.

When to use
- When failures cost far more than extra capacity.
- When estimates are noisy or data are limited.
- Skip if buffers waste scarce resources or cause harm.

Sizing rules of thumb
- Engineering: safety factor = design / expected load (common 1.5-4x).
- Investing: buy below ~2/3 of conservative intrinsic value.
- Ops: provision 20-50% then tune with metrics.

Engineering example
- Bridge needs 10,000 kg capacity.
- Design for 15,000-30,000 kg to cover material uncertainty, loads, and corrosion.

Investing example
- Buy a stock at a substantial discount to conservative value.
- The discount absorbs forecasting errors, downturns, and surprises.

Takeaway
- Build deliberate buffers.
- Size them to balance reduced failure risk against cost and adaptability.

Optionality
- Structure choices so wins are large and losses are small or known.
- Buy the right to scale after learning, not an all-in commitment.

Mechanism
- Cap loss with small sunk bets.
- Defer commitment until info arrives.
- Scale exposure after positive signals.

How it works
- Stage commitments (small -> larger follow-ons).
- Modular design to swap or extend later.
- Run cheap experiments to gather signals.

Example: seed investing
- Small seed check limits loss and buys info.
- If traction appears, follow-ons scale returns; if not, loss is contained.

Example: product tests
- Release feature to 5% behind a flag.
- Low cost caps downside; user metrics tell you to roll out or kill.

When to use
- High uncertainty with rare big upside.
- When info will arrive over time and you can scale exposure.
- Avoid if you must fully commit to coordinate teams.

Common misuse
- "Keeping options open" as procrastination.
- Perpetual pilots or no scale/exit rules waste optionality.

Prepare without predicting.
A mindset for rare, high-impact surprises you can't forecast but can survive or benefit from.

Definition
- Black swan: unexpected, huge impact, explained after the fact.
- Unknown unknowns: risks you cannot list before they occur.

Why they happen
- Complex systems + limited data hide tail risks.
- Feedback loops and model blind spots make some outcomes invisible until they arrive.

Core idea
- You can't predict every shock.
- Change system response: reduce fragility, add optionality, and limit ruin.

Practical levers
- Antifragility: tolerate or gain from stress
- Redundancy: spare capacity or buffers
- Diversity: avoid correlated failures
- Safe-to-fail experiments: small tests, fast learning

2008 financial crisis
- Models assumed mortgage risks were independent.
- Solution: build buffers and regulation to prevent systemic ruin, not predict each collapse.

COVID-19 lesson
- Efficiency-focused systems (just-in-time, no stockpiles) became brittle.
- Resilience = stockpiles, surge capacity, decentralised production.

Redundancy = duplicate capacity so one failure doesn't stop the system.
Slack = spare headroom to absorb variability and spikes.
Together: trade cost for lower risk and faster recovery.

Failures are random and often correlated.
Redundancy (parallel components) avoids single-point outages.
Slack (headroom) prevents queues and cascading slowdowns under load.

Design rule: Target capacity = peak load x safety factor.
Availability pattern: N+1 - add one unit so peak holds when one fails.
Treat +1 as operating capacity, not just a spare box.

Airlines use redundancy and slack.
Extra engines and spare aircraft keep flights running after failures.
Schedule buffers absorb small delays and protect connections.

Data centers use N+1 power and cooling.
Replicated servers and multi-AZ deployments give fast failover.
Runbooks help, but manual recovery is slower than automated backups.

Common mistakes:
- Buying identical duplicates without reducing correlated risks (same vendor/bug).
- Treating runbooks as a substitute for automated failover - slower under pressure.

How to apply:
1) Find critical single points of failure.
2) Decide recovery speed: minutes -> automated redundancy; hours -> runbooks + some slack.
3) Use N+1 and diversify suppliers.

We see winners, not failures.
Basing risk on visible survivors skews the true odds.

How it works
- Spot success stories
- Treat them as typical
- Ignore the unseen failures and you underestimate downside

Venture example
Media highlights unicorns.
Thousands of failed startups are invisible, so strategies that 'made a unicorn' can be misleading.

Why it matters
- Underestimates tail losses
- Encourages overconcentration
- Rewards headline-grabbing but fragile bets

Look for the hidden denominator
Ask: how many attempts failed? 
If 1 of 10 becomes a unicorn, average return per attempt can still be negative.

Adjust decisions
- Reconstruct full sample (survivors + failures)
- Model outcome distributions, not anecdotes
- Stress-test and price the chance of complete ruin

Takeaway
Don't dismiss winners, but always combine survivor stories with a rigorous count of failures and selection effects.

What are correlated failures?
- Multiple components fail together because they share a common cause.
- Not independent bad luck: a structural link (same vendor, design, supply, or process).

How they arise
- Shared dependencies or constraints (same provider, codebase, power, humans).
- When the shared element breaks, all dependents fail simultaneously.

Signals you have correlated-risk
- 'Diverse' parts using the same upstream provider or region
- Identical libraries, single-source raw materials, or uniform procedures across sites

Cloud outage example
- 2017 S3 control-plane bug took many services offline.
- Claimed multi-region redundancy failed because everyone relied on the same cloud control plane.

Financial example
- 2008: banks held 'diversified' mortgages but shared underwriting and securitization exposure.
- A single housing-price collapse created system-wide defaults.

Diversify effectively
- Use providers with different failure modes (different infra, ownership, supply).
- Separate critical dependencies across geography, regulation, design, and ops teams.

How to implement
1) Map dependencies top-to-bottom
2) Stress-test shared components
3) Classify failure modes
4) Mitigate per mode (alternates, fallbacks, manual plans)
5) Quantify and accept residual risk

title: Scenario planning: plausible futures, signposts, stress-test strategy

slides.text:
Design a few plausible futures
Pick signposts
Wind-tunnel your strategy to find robust and contingent options

Definition
Build a small set of internally consistent, plausible futures-not point forecasts-to reveal vulnerabilities and robust strategic options under uncertainty.

Core mechanics
1) Find the uncertainties that matter most
2) Combine into 3-5 coherent worlds
3) Map impacts and extract robust vs. contingent actions

Choosing uncertainties
Score trends by impact x uncertainty
Pick 2-3 axes that change trade-offs (e.g., regulation, tech adoption), not long lists of trends

Constructing scenarios
Pair opposite ends of your axes into clear narratives (e.g., regulated slow tech vs rapid adoption)
Explain market, customer, competitor behavior in each

Wind-tunneling
Run your strategy in each scenario
Ask which assumptions break and which KPIs fail
Spot capabilities that are robust or high-payoff but contingent

Signposts and triggers
List early indicators for each scenario (quantitative or qualitative)
Assign monitoring owners
Predefine trigger actions when signposts appear

Circuit breakers & tripwires
Practical stop-losses to keep small failures from becoming catastrophes.

Quick definitions
- Circuit breaker: automatic stop when a metric crosses a hard threshold.
- Tripwire: low-cost detector that alerts or escalates for human review.

Core mechanism
- Pick a few observable metrics tied to failures.
- Set thresholds and durations that separate normal variance from action.
- Define the response and clear ownership in advance.

Deployment example
- Metric: 5-minute error rate.
- Tripwire: >2% for 5 min -> notify on-call.
- Circuit breaker: >5% for 2 min -> automatic rollback and page engineer.

Financial example
- Stop-loss (circuit breaker): auto-sell if loss >8%.
- Stop-thinking (tripwire): flag at >3% loss and require reassessment before acting.

Auto vs human-in-loop
- Automatic: fast and bias-free but can be blunt and cause false stops.
- Human: nuanced and contextual but slower and subject to cognitive bias.

How to add one
- Map failure modes to ~3 metrics.
- Set thresholds, responses, and owners before deployment.
- Test with drills and monitor frequency; tune or decay thresholds as needed.

title: Risk of Ruin - size bets to survive

slides.text:
Risk of Ruin - size bets to survive

Survival can beat occasional big wins. Bet sizing decides if you stay in the game.

What is risk of ruin?

The chance repeated losses deplete capital so you can't continue. It's about staying able to play, not chasing peak returns.

How ruin happens

- Negative compounding: losses shrink base so future %-losses hurt more
- Streaks: randomness can cluster losses
- Position size sets the destruction speed

Math intuition

Betting fraction f means n losses multiply capital by (1-f)^n.
Small f tolerates streaks; large f can collapse capital in a few losses.

Kelly rule (intuition)

Bet ~ edge / variance. It maximizes geometric growth.
Many use fractional Kelly to cut short-term volatility and ruin risk.

VC example

Placing 30% of a fund into one deal risks fund survival if it fails.
Smaller stakes preserve capital to back later winners.

Trader example & takeaway

High leverage (10x) + 10% adverse move wipes you out.
Sizing trades to 1-2% lets you survive and realize positive edge.

Definition

- Principal delegates work to an agent who has different incentives and more info.
- Agent may act for personal gain, not the principal's goals.

Core mechanism

- Misaligned incentives + information asymmetry.
- Agent controls key actions; if payoffs diverge, agent favors private benefits over principal outcomes.

Design levers

- Align incentives: bonuses, equity, pay-for-performance.
- Reduce opacity: monitoring, reporting, audits.
- Limit discretion: rules, contracts, job design.

Corporate CEO example

- Shareholders want long-term value; CEOs may chase short-term metrics.
- Fixes: vesting, long-term incentives, clawbacks, stronger boards; beware single-metric bonuses.

Physician prescribing example

- Insurers seek cost-effective care; doctors know patient details and face patient or pharma pressures.
- Tools: guidelines, utilization review, selective pay-for-performance.

Common mistakes

- Excessive monitoring destroys trust and raises costs.
- Narrow metrics invite gaming.
- Removing discretion kills expert judgment and adaptability.

Practical checklist

1) Map decision rights and who benefits.
2) Spot private information and its effects.
3) Pick levers: incentives, targeted monitoring, or rules.
4) Test for gaming and distortions.
Goodhart's law: when a measure becomes a target, it stops being a reliable measure. Turning metrics into goals changes behavior and breaks the link to the underlying outcome.

Definition: converting a statistical measure into a policy or reward target reshapes agent behavior. People optimize the proxy itself, so the metric loses correlation with the true objective over time.

Mechanism:
- Pick a proxy because the true goal is hard to measure
- Reward agents on that proxy
- Agents optimize the proxy, causing gaming or proxy drift away from the goal

Why it matters:
- Corrupted metrics create perverse incentives and wasted effort
- Harm and blind spots often appear only after the metric is entrenched in decisions

Education example:
- Use test scores to judge schools
- Teachers teach to the test; narrow curricula and tactics raise scores while deep learning and critical thinking fall

Product example:
- Optimize daily active users with frequent notifications and low-quality hooks
- DAU rises but user satisfaction, content quality, and long-term retention decline
Takeaway: guard the metric, not just the target. Use multiple proxies, track guardrail metrics, and audit behavior so measures stay aligned with the real goal.

Moral hazard

Behavior changes when someone else bears the loss.
Protection (insurance, guarantees, limited liability) can make people take more risk.

Core mechanism

- Losses fall on another party
- Protector can't fully observe actions
- Decision-maker gains upside but faces less downside

Bank bailouts

If managers expect rescues they favor short-term profits and risky leverage.
Losses get socialized while gains stay private, raising systemic risk.

Insurance and maintenance

Full flood insurance can reduce a homeowner's incentive to invest in prevention.
With damage covered, prevention's marginal benefit falls and claims rise.

Observable patterns

- More risk-taking after protection
- Less investment in avoidance
- Cutting corners or choosing riskier strategies when downside is buffered

Mitigations

- Deductibles or co-pay to keep some downside with actor
- Performance pay tied to long-term outcomes
- Monitoring, reporting, and conditional or graduated support

Takeaway

Balance protections to prevent harm without removing incentives for prudent behavior.

Definition

One side knows quality or risk before a deal. That hidden info changes who participates, often leaving worse-quality or higher-risk parties in the market.

Mechanism

- Buyers set a price for average quality
- High-quality sellers exit when price is too low
- Average quality drops and prices fall further

Lemons market (Akerlof)

If buyers can't tell good from bad used cars, they pay an average price. Owners of good cars withdraw, leaving mostly lemons and a weak market.

Insurance

People know their health/risk better than insurers. Healthy people opt out of community rates, raising premiums and pushing more healthy people away.

Venture investing

Founders know startup quality. Low signals lead investors to low valuations, good teams seek other funding, leaving investors with weaker startups and poorer returns.

Screening vs signalling

- Screening: buyers verify - tests, checks, conditional contracts, warranties
- Signalling: sellers prove quality - guarantees, brand, costly actions only high-quality can afford

Takeaway

Hidden-quality problems can unravel markets. Fixes (screening or signalling) help but cost money and can shrink participation.

Why rational choices can harm everyone.
Structure, time, and retaliation can flip incentives so cooperation becomes possible.

The Prisoner's Dilemma
- Two players choose Cooperate or Defect.
- Mutual cooperation pays well; mutual defection pays worse.
- One-shot Nash equilibrium: both defect.

Why defection dominates in one shot
- For any fixed opponent move, defection gives equal or higher payoff.
- Individual incentive to defect conflicts with collective benefit of cooperation.

Levers that change outcomes
- Payoffs: change gains or losses.
- Information: observation and signaling.
- Repetition: future consequences alter today's incentives.

Repeated games effect
- Future interactions let players condition actions on history.
- If future cooperative gains exceed one-time defection gains, cooperation can be sustained.

Tit-for-tat (one example)
- Start cooperating, then mirror the opponent's last move.
- Simple, retaliatory, forgiving, transparent - it stabilizes cooperation in repeated play.

Takeaway
- Change the shadow of the future, information, or payoffs and you can turn defection into stable cooperation.

Group must pick one option without talking.
The obvious option that emerges is a focal point (Schelling point), guiding expectations.

- Payoff rises when people choose the same option.
- Focal point: a salient feature (convention, default, law) that makes one option stand out.

- People pick what they expect others to pick.
- Salience (history, visibility, authority, simplicity) signals common expectation and breaks symmetry.

- VHS and Betamax were both workable formats.
- VHS won because studios and rental stores coordinated on it, creating availability that reinforced adoption.

- EU rules and major manufacturers aligned on USB-C.
- Legal signal plus producer convergence reduced consumer friction and collapsed rival ecosystems.

- Coordination tax: extra cost when groups fail to share a standard.
- Effects: incompatible files, multiple payment apps, and mismatched APIs raise switching and integration costs.

- Reduce choices: limit options or set a default.
- Make one option salient: branding, visible adoption.
- Commit and subsidize: standards, contracts, early-adopter incentives to overcome inertia.

Your alternatives are your leverage.
Better, credible options let you demand more or walk; weak or invisible alternatives leave you conceding.

BATNA defined
- Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement: your plan if talks fail
- Reservation value (walk-away): the minimal deal equal to your BATNA

Core mechanism
- Strong, credible BATNA increases bargaining power
- Weak BATNA forces acceptance of worse terms or concessions

Compute leverage
- Compare BATNA value to the deal (outcomes, costs, timing)
- Add credibility: can the other side see and believe your alternative?

Job offer example
- Candidate has a written $120k offer (strong BATNA)
- Employer offers $100k; candidate can push for $120k+ or walk

M&A example
- Seller with multiple bidders commands higher price and tighter covenants
- Buyer with alternate targets gains symmetric leverage if seller lacks options

Asymmetric info risk
- Hiding or bluffing a BATNA can win short-term gains
- But being caught destroys credibility and often ends the deal

Make promises believable by making backtracking costly or impossible.
Commitments turn vague promises into reliable signals others can act on.

Definition
- A credible commitment converts a promise into a real constraint.
- Breaking it imposes observable costs or hands control/value to a neutral party.

Mechanism
- Raise the cost of reneging or
- Remove the decision from the promisor.
Both change payoffs so partners trust future behavior.

Burn the boats (irreversibility)
- Cortés scuttled his ships to remove retreat as an option.
- That irreversible act increased his men's commitment to the campaign.

Hostages & escrow (third-party enforcement)
- Place value with a neutral custodian contingent on behavior.
- Example: merger escrow holds funds for indemnities until risks resolve.

When to use
- Low trust, misaligned incentives, or when future actions must be credible.
- Avoid if you need flexibility or when commitments are costly relative to gains.

Payoff
- Use commitments to make promises enforceable and cooperation possible.
- Design them so costs are clear, observable, and hard to reverse.

Signaling: an actor uses an observable action to reveal an unobservable trait.
Screening: a receiver designs tests or steps to separate types when traits are hidden.

Mechanism
- A good signal costs more for low-quality types than high-quality ones.
- Cheap talk (costless claims) is ignored; screening makes desired types self-select.

Why it matters
- Cuts hiring, investment, and pricing errors by turning hidden traits into decisions.
- Poor designs fuel credential inflation and exclude capable people.

Education as a signal
- Degrees cost time and effort, so they correlate with ability and commitment.
- When many earn the same degree, its informational value falls and credential arms races start.

VC screening funnel
- Stages (apply -> pitch -> due diligence -> term sheet) raise time and disclosure costs.
- Weaker founders drop out; stronger founders persist because payoffs justify the costs.

Warranties, deposits, trials
- Long warranties signal confidence since failures impose costs on sellers.
- Deposits and trials deter abusers by imposing upfront costs or commitments.

Common failures
- Relying on cheap talk anyone can fake.
- Overusing costly signals that gatekeep talent.
- Mistaking correlation for causation.
- Arms races that raise costs without improving quality.

title: Collective Action Problem
subtopics: Small group vs large group dynamics

slides.text:
Definition

- Individuals withhold effort despite a shared benefit.
- Group outcome is worse than if enough people cooperated.

Core mechanism

- Private payoff ≠ social payoff.
- Costly contributions but shared benefits lead people to free-ride, especially as groups grow.

Key variables

- Rivalry/excludability of the good (public vs private).
- Group size, visibility, enforcement, heterogeneity, and repetition.

Why it helps

- Predicts when goods are under-provided or coordination fails.
- Guides fixes: change incentives, boost visibility, add selective benefits, or enforce rules.

Example: small vs large groups

- Neighborhood watch: small, visible, repeated-social sanctions sustain effort.
- Climate action: vast, anonymous-free-riding prevails without institutions.

Design patterns

- Make contributions visible.
- Offer selective incentives.
- Enforce rules, form subgroups, or change payoff structure (matching, thresholds).

Caution

- Don't blame individuals for system failures.
- Visibility alone can backfire (privacy or performative behavior).

Shared-resource problem

Multiple users share a resource. Each gains by taking more, but the harm is spread across all. Rational choices by individuals can destroy the resource.

Core mechanism

Private benefit from extra use exceeds private cost. Social cost rises but isn't paid by the taker. Use escalates until the resource collapses.

Concrete case: lobster ground

Fishers add traps to raise personal catch. Catch per trap falls and juveniles die. The stock collapses next season.

Typical (failing) responses

- Race to extract faster (noncooperative)
- Wait for others to restrain themselves (free-rider)
- Try private exclusion where possible (partial fix)

High-leverage fixes

- Align incentives: property rights, quotas, tradable permits
- Change payoffs: taxes or usage fees
- Enable coordination: agreements, monitoring, sanctions
- Regenerate: closures, reserves

Design checklist

- Measure stock and flow
- Map winners and payers
- Pick instruments that create local accountability
- Build monitoring and credible enforcement
- Allow adaptive updates with new data

Common mistakes

- Treating one-off overuse like chronic underinvestment
- Relying on market rights when measurement is poor
- Imposing top-down rules without local buy-in

Trust = willingness to be vulnerable based on expectations of others.
Reciprocity = returning favors, cooperation, or sanctions that confirm those expectations and enable stable cooperation.

Three-step mechanism:
1) Signal: a low-cost action reveals intent.
2) Response: the other party reciprocates.
3) Reinforcement: repetition turns responses into durable norms.

Key levers to shift outcomes:
- Interaction frequency (more repeats -> more cooperation).
- Visibility (public acts build reputation).
- Signal clarity and retaliation costs.

Vendor-buyer example:
Supplier gives a small early discount (signal).
Buyer pays and orders more (reciprocity).
Repeated cycles create prioritized, low-friction partnership.

Peer marketplace example:
Hosts add thoughtful touches (signal).
Guests leave timely honest reviews (reciprocity).
Public reviews lower future trust costs for everyone.

When it breaks:
No repetition, asymmetric info, anonymity, or low visibility stop reciprocity.
Then trust collapses or needs external enforcement (contracts, deposits, audits).

How to apply:
Design for repeat interactions, make cooperative acts visible,
lower costs of small positive signals, and add cheap verification when stakes are high.

Definition

A bottleneck is the single resource, policy, or step that limits a system's throughput. Improving non-constraints won't raise output; only changes at the bottleneck lift the system ceiling.

Mechanism

System throughput = capacity of the bottleneck. Work piles up upstream (queues) and downstream may starve. Lead time is driven by waiting at or for the constraint.

How to spot it

- Persistent queues or the earliest recurring delay
- Stage at ~100% utilization while others have slack
- Where work is always backlogged or wait times spike

Rule 1 - Subordinate to the constraint

- Schedule and feed work to protect the bottleneck
- Avoid overproducing upstream; keep a steady, predictable supply to the constraint

Rule 2 - Elevate the constraint

- First exploit it: remove waste and ensure reliability
- Only add capacity where it actually limits throughput; otherwise you just shift the constraint

Drum-Buffer-Rope (TOC)

- Drum = pace set by the bottleneck
- Buffer = limited inventory/time before the drum to protect it
- Rope = upstream release tied to the drum; lowers lead time and WIP

Factory example

CNC does 20 parts/hr, painting and inspection 30/hr - CNC is the constraint. Fixes: ensure continuous feed, cut CNC downtime, add capacity or redesign the CNC step.

title: Little's Law: Turn WIP into predictable lead time

slides.text:
Little's Law links WIP, throughput, and lead time.
Manage WIP and throughput to predict how long work spends in your system.

Statement
- In steady state: average Lead time (L) = average WIP ÷ average throughput (λ).
- Common form: Lead time = WIP / Throughput.

Why it works
- In steady state, items enter and leave at the same rate.
- More WIP with constant throughput means each item spends proportionally more time in the system.

Units matter
- WIP (items) ÷ Throughput (items per time) = Lead time (time).
- Use the same item definition and matching time window for throughput.

Manufacturing example
- 200 units WIP and 50 units/day throughput.
- Lead time = 200 ÷ 50 = 4 days. Cut WIP or raise throughput to shorten lead time.

Software example
- 40 active tickets and 10 tickets/week throughput.
- Lead time ≈ 40 ÷ 10 = 4 weeks. Pulling more work raises lead time unless throughput increases.

Takeaway
- Little's Law gives a reliable bridge from WIP and throughput to expected lead time.
- Control WIP for the simplest, fastest way to shorten lead times.

title: Queueing & Variability: utilization, delay, and buffering

slides.text:
Definition

- Work arrives, limited capacity processes it.
- Variability = random ups and downs in arrivals or service.
- Together they set delays and needed buffers.

Core mechanism

- As utilization (arrival ÷ capacity) nears 100%, small bursts create long queues.
- No spare capacity means backlogs take long to clear.

Compact formula

- Little's Law: L = λW (items = rate x time).
- Delay blows up as ρ -> 1; e.g., M/M/1 delay ≈ (ρ/(1-ρ))·(1/μ).

Concrete example

- 10 customers/min arrive, 1 cashier serves 11/min (ρ≈0.91).
- Short bursts make long lines; adding a cashier cuts waits dramatically.

Where to buffer

- Add capacity: more servers or faster service.
- Time buffers: slack, lead time.
- Inventory/WIP or reduce variability: smoothing and standardization.

Trade-off

- Spare capacity costs money but slashes delay.
- Too little capacity yields long waits and unpredictable throughput.
- Balance capacity cost vs cost of delay.

Practical checks

1) Measure utilization at the bottleneck.
2) Measure arrival and service variance.
3) Simulate or use simple queue models - averages can mislead.

What is a feedback loop?
- System output feeds back as input
- Reinforcing loops amplify change
- Balancing loops resist change toward a goal

Mechanism
- action -> effect -> sensing -> response -> action
- If response boosts the action it's reinforcing; if it opposes it, it's balancing
- Delays create instability or oscillation

When to apply this model
- Outcomes keep moving with no single cause
- Policies produce the opposite effect
- Performance cycles (boom/bust, churn spikes)

Reinforcing loop (virality)
- Each user invites others
- More users -> more invites -> faster growth
- Slow start can flip to rapid acceleration at critical mass

Balancing loop (thermostat)
- Sensor reads temperature
- Heater turns on/off to approach setpoint
- The loop resists deviation from the goal

Delays cause oscillation (traffic lights)
- Signals react to current congestion
- Driver reaction delays cause overcorrection
- Result: stop-and-go waves instead of smooth flow

Fixes that backfire (hospital example)
- Shorter stays intended to cut readmissions
- Early discharges return sicker patients
- The policy ignored recovery balancing loops and increased readmissions

Stock-and-Flow thinking: treat quantity and rate as separate levers.
Control the stock (what you have) and the flows (what changes it) to avoid surprises.

Stock = quantity accumulated over time (inventory, users, cash, CO2).
Flow = rate in or out that changes the stock. Stocks = prior stock + integral(inflows - outflows).

Changing a flow changes the stock only over time.
Impact depends on stock size and delays: same flow change moves small stocks fast but large stocks slowly.

Bathtub analogy: water level = stock.
Faucet = inflow, drain = outflow. Small faucet tweaks barely raise a big tub; boosting drain can drop level quickly if outflow > inflow.

Retail: finished-goods stock moves by production (inflow) and sales (outflow).
Finance: cash balance is a stock; slow receivables delay converting revenue into usable cash.

1) Map stocks and their inflows/outflows.
2) Choose lever: change flows or build buffers.
3) Quantify magnitudes and delays - small flow changes won't move big stocks fast.

Don't treat stocks as instantly adjustable by changing a flow.
Example: hiring freezes don't instantly cut headcount costs - headcount falls only as people leave, causing lags and oscillations.

title: Single Points of Failure - map, mitigate, design resilient systems

slides.text:
Single Point of Failure (SPOF)
- Any single component whose failure stops required functionality.
- Define by impact: if it can make your system unavailable, it's a SPOF.

Why SPOFs matter
- Dependencies concentrate risk and cause cascades.
- Common mechanisms: blocking calls, shared state, centralized coordination, or single-owner ops.

Map SPOFs systematically
- Inventory user-facing features and trace each request path.
- Mark single-instance components or single-operator tasks and score impact by duration x severity.

Core mitigations
- Redundancy: N>1 independent instances and separate power/network.
- Failover: health checks, quorum, leader election and state transfer.
- Decouple: async queues and graceful degradation.

Real-world: DNS as SPOF
- DNS provider outage makes healthy servers unreachable.
- Mitigate: multi-provider DNS, short TTLs, health checks and automated failover or IP fallback.

Common mistakes
- Copying misconfigurations onto replicas creates shared SPOFs.
- Too much identical redundancy fails on the same bug.
- Single-human ops and undocumented steps remain SPOFs.

Takeaway
- Find the top SPOFs, add diversity and automation, test failover, and remove single-human operational dependencies.

Core claim

Modularity splits systems into components with clear interfaces.
Coupling is how much components depend on each other.
Good modularity keeps changes and failures local.

Why it works

Boundaries limit where change and failure propagate.
Clear contracts let you replace, test, scale, or fail parts independently.

Kinds of coupling

- Data coupling: sharing raw structures (tight).
- Protocol coupling: stable APIs/messages (looser).
- Temporal/control coupling: sync or one component driving another (fragile).

Example: e-commerce search

If catalog shares DB schema, catalog changes break search (data coupling).
If search uses a versioned API returning stable docs, teams can evolve independently.

Monolith vs modular

Monolith: simpler ops and low latency but changes often need global coordination.
Modular: isolates failures and enables independent deploys but costs API design and observability.

Common mistakes

- Over-modularizing small systems adds ops overhead.
- Thin contracts that leak internals (IDs, raw rows).
- Implicit coupling via shared libs or databases.

Practical checklist

- Define minimal, versioned interfaces and prefer API/message contracts.
- Encapsulate data; expose intent not structure.
- Add health/fallbacks and track callers, schema churn, deploy blast radius.

What is batch size?

- Number of units grouped for processing, deploy, or delivery.
- Batching cuts setup/coordination costs but raises waiting, feedback delay, and rework risk.

Core mechanism

- Setup cost S happens once per batch.
- Per-item cost c scales with items.
- Delay/holding cost D grows as items wait; optimal N balances S vs marginal D.

Simple model

- Cost per item ≈ c + S/N + D(N).
- Small N -> big S/N. Large N -> big D(N).
- Minimum where reducing S/N equals added delay cost.

Manufacturing example

- 30-min setup makes S big; 1 part wastes setup, 100 parts lower S/N.
- But larger WIP raises defect risk and rework cost, favoring smaller batches if defects are costly.

Software deployments

- Big releases cut coordination per release (lower S).
- But bugs affect many users and slow feedback; small frequent releases shorten loops unless overhead is high.

When to pick size

- Favor small batches: high uncertainty, costly delays, expensive rework, need fast learning.
- Favor large batches: huge fixed setup, stable predictable process, or dangerous switching.

How to choose in practice

- Measure S and average item wait; reduce S with automation.
- Experiment downward: track defects, cycle time, coordination.
- Use max-batch or time flush policies, not rigid counts.

title: WIP limits: expose and fix bottlenecks

slides.text:
WIP limits = explicit caps on items allowed in a workflow state.
They make hidden overloads and context-switch costs visible so teams must manage work, not just start more.

Why it works
- Caps reveal bottlenecks as upstream queues grow
- Pushes teams to finish, not start
- Reduces handoffs and switching, shortening time-in-system

How to set limits
- Set per-stage, not one global cap
- Rule of thumb: WIP ≈ people doing that stage x 1-2
- Start low, observe flow, raise if idle time is frequent

Pull-system fit
- Downstream stages pull only when under their WIP limit
- Intake follows real capacity, preventing specialist overloads and uncontrolled starts

Swarming vs multitasking
- Multitasking: many concurrent items -> high switching, longer lead times
- Swarming: focus a few items to done -> faster cycles
- WIP limits nudge teams toward swarming

Real example
- 6-person team set 'In Review' WIP = 3
- When queue hit 3, devs paired on reviews and removed blockers
- Review wait dropped from 2.5 days to ~8 hours

Metrics to watch
- Track cycle time, throughput, and per-stage queue size
- If queues exceed WIP: remove blockers or reallocate people - avoid reflexively raising the limit

title: Maintenance debt: principal, interest, when to pay

slides.text:
Maintenance debt = deferred upkeep that saves now but costs later.
It accumulates "interest" as outages, friction, and complexity increase over time.

Definition
- Postponed upkeep of code, hardware, or processes.
- Short-term productivity preserved; long-term operating cost, failure risk, and change speed worsen.

How it grows
- Defer tasks (patches, refactors, calibrations).
- Small defects compound into interactions and brittleness.
- Ops friction and outages rise, making fixes larger later.

Principal vs interest
- Principal: the work you skipped (replace parts, refactor a module).
- Interest: ongoing costs-slower delivery, more bugs, emergency fixes, churn.

Preventive vs reactive
- Preventive: scheduled small investments (patching, cleaning, refactors) that lower long-term cost.
- Reactive: fix-after-failure-unpredictable, costly, and prone to cascades.

Interest-rate intuition
- Each deferred task multiplies future work by factor r per time unit.
- If r>1 cost grows fast; coupling, poor visibility raise r. Tests and monitoring lower it.

Timing takeaway
- Choose low-change windows to refactor when expected saved interest exceeds the refactor cost.
- Watch for feature freezes, scheduled downtime, or light sprints.

title: Correlation vs Causation: Spot and Test Real Causes

slides.text:
Definition

- Correlation: X and Y move together.
- Causation: changing X produces a change in Y.
- Correlation is necessary but not sufficient for causation.

Why correlation fools you

- Confounder Z drives both X and Y.
- Reverse causality: Y -> X.
- Coincidence or data mining produces spurious links.

Ice cream vs drownings

- Ice cream sales ↔ drownings.
- True cause: warm weather increases swimming and ice-cream demand.
- Acting on the correlation misses the real driver.

Reverse causality example

- Hospitals: more staff ↔ higher mortality.
- Reality: higher deaths lead hospitals to add staff.
- Correlation flips the causal arrow.

Signals that hint at causality

- Dose-response pattern.
- Cause precedes effect in time.
- Effect holds after controlling plausible confounders.

How to test causality

- Best: randomized controlled trials.
- Alternatives: natural experiments, IV, DiD, regression discontinuity.
- All require explicit assumption checks.

Quick pre-claim checks

- Add plausible confounders: does effect vanish?
- Reverse timing: does the effect persist?
- Use placebo outcomes and subgroup/dose tests.

Definition

- Confounding: an unmeasured Z causes both X and Y.
- Omitted-variable bias: leaving Z out makes X->Y estimates systematically wrong.

Mechanism

- If Z -> X and Z -> Y, observed X-Y mix Z's effect with X's.
- Naive estimates confound causal effect and shared influence.

Intuition

- Z is a hidden thermostat.
- When Z rises it pushes up both X (heater use) and Y (room temp), creating a false X->Y link.

Real-world example

- Early studies tied coffee (X) to heart disease (Y).
- Smoking (Z) was common among coffee drinkers and caused heart disease, biasing the coffee estimate.

Detecting confounding

- Use theory to list plausible Z's.
- Test if candidate Z correlates with both X and Y.
- See if estimates shift when you add controls.

Fixes and trade-offs

- Measure and adjust for Z (regression, matching) - still vulnerable to unmeasured Z.
- Randomize X if possible.
- Use IVs, DiD, RDD, or fixed effects under their assumptions.

Practical rules

- Measure likely confounders first.
- Show how estimates change with controls.
- State identification assumptions and prefer designs breaking selection from assignment.

title: Causal diagrams (DAGs): define before you decorate

slides.text:
What is a DAG?
- Nodes = variables, arrows = direct causal effects.
- Acyclic: you can't follow arrows and return.
- DAGs state causal assumptions, not correlations.

Why structure matters
- Paths show how associations arise.
- Which variables to condition on follows path rules.
- Blocked vs open paths determine bias in causal estimates.

Backdoor criterion (practical)
- To estimate A -> Y, find Z that blocks every path into A.
- Adjusting for Z removes confounding without opening new biasing paths.

Example: smoking and lung cancer
- Smoking -> Tar -> Cancer.
- SES -> Smoking and SES -> Cancer creates a backdoor via SES.
- Adjust for SES to control confounding.

Collider trap
- A -> Hospital <- B (hospital is a collider).
- Conditioning on the collider opens a spurious A-B link.
- Don't adjust for colliders unless you want that bias.

Real misuse: COVID studies
- Conditioning on hospitalization (a collider of severity and care-seeking)
- Induced bias can reverse apparent treatment effects (collider stratification).

Takeaway: mediation vs moderation
- Mediation: A -> M -> Y, decomposes direct vs indirect effects.
- Moderation: W changes A -> Y's size. Different questions; choose analysis to match the question.

title: Randomized Controlled Trials - the cleanest way to learn cause -> effect

slides.text: Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs): random assignment to isolate causal effect

Definition: An RCT assigns subjects randomly to at least two groups (treatment and control) so that, in expectation, the groups differ only by the treatment. Differences in outcomes can then be attributed to the treatment rather than confounders.

Mechanism: Randomization breaks systematic associations between all pre-existing factors (observed and unobserved) and treatment. That creates exchangeable groups; therefore the average difference in outcomes estimates the causal effect (the average treatment effect).

Key assumptions: (1) Random assignment is properly implemented; (2) Stable unit treatment value assumption (SUTVA) - no interference between units and no multiple versions of treatment; (3) No differential attrition or missingness that depends on potential outcomes.

Practical setup: (a) Define a clear estimand (what causal effect you want); (b) Choose population and eligibility; (c) Specify randomization scheme (simple, stratified, cluster); (d) Pre-specify primary outcome and analysis plan; (e) Run trial, monitor fidelity, record deviations.

Randomization checks: compare baseline covariates across arms to detect implementation errors (not to 'adjust' if balance is slightly off). Large imbalances suggest problems with the random process, blocked randomization or enrollment mistakes - investigate, don't overfit post hoc.

Intent-to-treat (ITT) vs Per-protocol: ITT estimates the effect of assignment (policy effect) and preserves randomization; per-protocol estimates effect of actually receiving treatment but is biased if compliance correlates with outcomes. Use IV methods (assignment as instrument) to estimate complier average causal effect when compliance is imperfect.

title: Regression to the Mean
subtopics: How to avoid false causal claims

slides.text:
Definition

Extreme observations tend to be followed by more typical ones because part of an extreme result is random noise, so later measurements often sit closer to the average.

Mechanism

Observed = true signal + noise
Extreme values often include large noise, and new noise is independent, so extremes usually don't repeat.

Math intuition

Picking top scorers mixes real skill and luck.
On repeat, luck fades, so the group's average falls toward the overall mean.

Sports example - sophomore slump

A rookie posts huge stats.
Some of that was luck (matchups, variance). Year two evens luck out, so stats often drop.

Medicine example

Patients treated after a severe flare often improve naturally.
If you start a new therapy then, improvement may reflect natural recovery, not the treatment.

How to spot it

Look for selection on extremes, noisy measurements, and follow-up moving toward the population mean without a lasting mechanism.

How to avoid false claims

Use controls or randomization, compare to similar non-selected groups, or get repeated baselines or hierarchical corrections.

title: Measurement error & noise: how recorded data deviates from truth
subtopics: When better data makes worse decisions

slides.text:
**What it is**

- Measurement error = recorded value minus true quantity.
- Systematic error (bias) shifts results; noise is unpredictable scatter.

**How it forms**

- Observed = true + systematic error + random noise + processing error.
- Bias shifts all readings; noise varies across repeats; processing adds rounding/aggregation faults.

**How to spot them**

- Repeatability (same setup) exposes random noise.
- Reproducibility (different setups) reveals systematic bias; residual plots show mean shift, heteroskedasticity, or drift.

**Real example: glucose meter**

- Meter can be precise (low noise) yet biased if miscalibrated.
- Repeatable readings may all be 10% high versus lab standard, altering dosing decisions.

**When more data hurts**

- Adding high-frequency noisy inputs can destabilize control policies or overfit models.
- Historical backtests tuned to noise fail when noise patterns change.

**Common mistakes**

- Treating repeatability as truth: consistent but biased.
- Assuming averaging removes bias: it doesn't.
- Ignoring context: operators and environment change errors.

**Actions to take**

- Run repeatability + reproducibility tests.
- Calibrate against standards and log drift.
- Denoise before decisioning and simulate biased/noisy scenarios.

Leading indicators move before an outcome and signal causal inputs.
Lagging indicators measure the outcome after it happens and confirm results.

- Lead: act early and run experiments.
- Lag: validate whether those actions changed the outcome.

Causality checklist for a good lead:
- Precedes the outcome in time
- Has a plausible mechanism
- Responds faster (sensitive)
- Predicts specifically (few false positives)

SaaS example:
- Lead: weekly trial-to-activation rate
- Lag: monthly recurring revenue (MRR)
Raise activation, then watch MRR later to confirm impact.

Indicator decay:
- Predictive power drifts as product, season, or market change
- Monitor rolling lead->lag correlation and recalibrate metrics

Common misuse:
- Tracking raw signups (volume) that lack quality
- Leads improve but revenue doesn't-fix by shifting to downstream or quality-filtered metrics
Payoff: Use fast, plausible leads to act; use lags to prove results and update your indicators.

Proxy metric: a measurable sign used instead of a hard-to-measure target.
Metric validity: how well that sign actually tracks the thing you care about.

How it works
- Map a construct (e.g., satisfaction) to observable signals (NPS, defects).
- Validity needs stable, specific, and causal linkage, not just correlation.

Signals a proxy is valid
- Predicts future target outcomes
- Moves when the construct moves, not when confounders change
- Resists simple gaming
Use independent, converging measures.

Practical checklist
1) Define the construct precisely
2) State assumptions linking proxy->construct
3) Validate externally (studies, experiments)
4) Triangulate with an orthogonal measure
5) Monitor drift

Example: conversion rate for product-market fit
- Early signal of demand
- Can be distorted by paid traffic or promotions
- Triangulate with retention and customer interviews

Misuse case: engagement as well-being proxy
- Optimize clicks/likes -> short-term engagement rises
- Long-term satisfaction and mental health can fall
- Engagement is confounded by sensational content

Limits & tradeoffs
- Simple proxies are cheap but brittle
- Complex composites improve validity but reduce interpretability
- Expect diminishing returns on validation effort

A/B tests can show a 'winning' variant that isn't a true causal win.
Small-sample noise, multiple looks, or traffic quirks can create false positives that vanish after rollout.

Setup
- Ecommerce team randomized visitors to a redesign versus control for two weeks.
- On day 3 a clear lift appears; PM stops the test and ships the redesign.

Peeking / p-hacking
- Repeatedly checking interim results raises the chance of a random spike.
- Stopping early on a noisy spike often captures transient noise, not a stable effect.

Multiple comparisons
- Running many variants (colors, headlines, banners) without correction inflates false positives.
- One variant will often look significant purely by chance.

Novelty and seasonality
- Early boosts can reflect novelty or a temporary traffic mix (weekend shoppers, promotions).
- Those effects fade or reverse when user mix normalizes.

Concrete example
- Streaming app saw +4% conversions after 48 hours and rolled out the change.
- Two months later revenue fell - early lift came from weekend sports fans over-represented in the test window.

False-positive math
- Each test at alpha=0.05 has a 5% false-positive risk.
- Peek repeatedly or test many variants and familywise false-positive rate rises toward 1 - (1-0.05)^n.

What it is
- Measure model on data never used to build or tune it
- Gives a realistic estimate of future performance, not memorized quirks of training data

Why it works
- Training fits patterns, including dataset-specific noise
- Holding data aside forces an independent test of whether learned patterns generalize

Minimal protocol
- Split into training and untouched holdout before any modeling
- Use k-fold CV on training for selection and tuning
- Evaluate final model once on the holdout only

Example: readmission model
- Train and tune on early records with CV
- Reserve last 6 months as holdout (time split)
- Test once on holdout to estimate production performance

Common leakage mistakes
- Feature leakage: include future-derived variables (e.g., post-discharge codes)
- Validation leakage: compute scalers on full data
- Peeking at holdout and re-tuning it

When to use time splits
- If data are ordered by time or cohort, train on past and test on future
- Random splits overstate performance when distributions drift over time

Statistical precautions
- Report uncertainty (CI or bootstrap) on holdout metrics
- If many variants tried, use nested CV or correct for multiple comparisons
